author: @1Chimonger
text:
It’d take a generously large field of them…but, only if you need to fully run an average tract home.   Estimators said our South roof could only hold 12.5 kW of solar panels (no battery bank), & that was not enuf to run an average 1400s.f. all-electric house, when heat came on in winter….we’d still be paying a utility bill in winter, & maybe an AC bill in summer. 
But that was mandated grid-tied panels only.  At about $35K.  

Utility co’s have been mandating home solar in municipalities, to be grid-tied, to take a load off of utility green-mandates—law got passed some years ago, mandating they must get something like 75% of the energy from renewables…?….impossible….& all that is tied in with terrible deteriorated grid systems, & things like BPA (Bonneville Power Assoc.)taking utility co’s to court to force them to help repay the trillion$ in debt BPA mistakes in the 1990s, caused. 
Some folks might do better by setting-up a solar trailer + battery bank, with a grid-interface, so it’s portable, & wire-into the house, some off-grid dedicated circuits, or, use it as a backup generator. 
All the solar roof systems now, are out of the owners’ pockets (forget the Fed.Incentives!—only those rich enuf to Itemize on taxes, can get those!!).  
Mandating them grid-tied, puts those systems at owners’ expense, NOT the utilities’, for installs, & maintenance, replace & repair costs—so, you’ll need a plush savings account to build-up to afford that, in roughly 10 to 40 years….when the owners who bot them, will often be in retirement on fixed-incomes.   
For many, there is NO real ROI (return on investment), because of repaying about the price of a (now) modest car takes so long for most folks.  
And, there’s NO protections against govt later demanding or taxing the renewables You paid dearly to install—govt keeps an updates National survey (by satlleites), of where all the solar installs are. 

So,  IF ok with generating more modest amounts…say…to charge to battery boxes such as a Goal Zero or any of several brands like it, the pendulum generators could be mounted along fences in back yard, & along the backyard side of the roof (out of public view), for instance. 

Our GZ 1400 watt battery box has only been recharging at the rate of about 60 watts from wall socket….but that has been running 2 cold boxes (about 200 watts if both kick-on at same time), for several years—taking our cold storage off-grid.  
It could probably easily handle our housemates’ fridge during an outage, if that got timer’d off several hours per nite…but since that one uses over 700 watts every time it’s running, it would be better to have a few hundred watts of  solar panels—or a bunch of these pendulum generators—to charge an add-on battery as well.  
We have 2 simple chest freezers—freezers have more insulation than an upright fridge, & use massively less energy…especially how ours are set up (one is used as a fridge, the other as freezer; both limited running on mechanical timers). 


IF we could mount ..enuf.. of these generators, none would be the wiser, from sky watchers, & they wouldn’t look like much to most thieves, these are MUCH less costly to make or replace, they are basic simple—that’s a LOT of benefits, NONE of the solar or wind gadgets can do.   
& we could at the very least, run 3 or 4 cold-boxes in this house off-grid; & Maybe the modem & computers each uses, as well.

author: @iangregory3719
text:
Fantastic design, elegant in its simplicity. 
Not 100% in agreement on the integral hinge though.  As a proof of concept during the R & D phase, yes, no problem,  but, for mass production it's not the best long term solution. 
 This is the sort of device that ,for production, would suit injection moulding.  Simplified into a few basic components,  thousands of parts kits could be made per day, and even assembled on site. The few, extra operations such as drillings would be jig based. For a long service life, a more robust hinge would be required.  Me, I'd probably opt for something like PTFE  bushes with a pin made of Delrin rod - banged out on a CNC by the million - that would be very low friction ,and rain water would help lubricate it. 
 It could be made from wood, though that will eventually rot. This would be ideal for reground plastics where aesthetics and high strength are not needed. 

 Oh, and thanks Rob, I once worked in plastics R&D, your videos have got me thinking about things in a way I hadn't done in years...👍.

author: @justtinkering6713
text: I just realized a problem with this idea. If you have more than one of these they wouldn't all produce a positive polarity voltage at the same time, so if you wire them in series you would expect the voltages to add together.  Some would be producing a negative voltage. if they weren't in sync , they would null each other.  Current can't go both ways in a conductor at the same instant. Maybe each one could be connected to a capacitor, through a rectifier .So a field of these would create chaotic blips and virtually no power to speak of.

If there is a steady decent airflow (with gusts on top of it), the stalk would bend and create a situation where the pendulum doesn't swing between the coils any-more. The pendulum would go back and forth but the coils would be out of reach.
Would it be possible to place the coils on their own pendulum so that they are always directly beneath the hinge of the magnet-pendulum?

author: @peterfelecan3639
text:
Have you tried your demonstration  inside, i.e., with no wind but just your body micro-adjustments ? You'll see that it still oscillates. What your are seeing outside is in part due to your body adjusting to the wind in keeping its equilibrium. For a more rigorous test you should install it on a stable support, which your body is not.
Another issue is the fact that you can only take advantage of the oscillations in one axis, let say frontal, but the lateral ones are not converting anything; for alleviating this issue you need at least a 2DOF pendulum instead of 1DOF.

author: @DFPercush
text:
This reminds me of those tuned mass dampers that they use in skyscrapers to keep the wind motion down. But you don't want to dampen the motion in this application, you want it to resonate as much as possible. I think in order to achieve that you'd want the length of the flapper to be 1 / (2 * any odd number >1) the length of the stalk, so 1/6, 1/10, 1/14 etc.  (edit: The ratio of the timing should be 1/6 or 1/10, but that may not correspond to length, because the two oscillators have different types of restoring force, one by gravity, one by spring tension).
    My main concern with putting thousands or millions of these in the field, is the cost of the magnets. How many watts are you getting per mass of Neodymium? Maybe someone will start mass producing cheap NiFeP magnets in the mean time.

author: @buffplums
text: Tech point if you connect them in series … a couple of issues… if one breaks you lose the lot also, if you get say half of them in opposition to the other half, worse case scenario is you get cancellation. So what I would suggest is to put all the devices in parallel but have a blocking diode on each item. Ok you are generating current rather than voltage but that’s no problem, if you take the output into a Buck Boost converter then the volts and amps are taken care of.

text: Interesting concept 👍.  I still feel there is lot more energy left untapped. The tricky part is variable wind loading(both direction and strength) while still keeping the twist to the optimal level. May be the oscillating leaf could change direction as it springs back, to maintain smaller cross section against wind?  Also another issue will be the total efficiency of several thousands of these getting converted to real useful power. Just some 💭

replies.author: @aware2action
replies.text:
​​@ThinkingandTinkering  Glad that you asked, Eventhough I don't have detailed solution, you could derive one based on the following:
1) It is best to use part of the untapped energy to increase efficiency further. One way is to use it to align the oscillating paddle, to return to a 90 degree orientation on the return swing.  This also
will keep the swing optimal, and pass on the excess wind force, that can be converted by other members. There are a few other methods of doing it, again the guiding principle is to keep complexity to least, additional cost to minimum.
Also treat the available wind force as volumetric than planar.
2) It is far more efficient, to store the energy without conversion to electricity until a certain threshold is reached. Say converting kinetic energy to potential energy and then a burst conversion to electricity, when it reaches the threshold.
The guiding principle is to keep it mechanical, while adapting to existing design without much modifications.
3)Combining the outputs into usable energy is the trickiest. This could involve couple of things: using less copper(single wire transmission) with common ground.
The output of each unit needs to be cascaded intelligently(the non productive members will identify themselves as a short). There is some nifty low power electronics involved, I could help with that if needed. The idea is to increase the collective output voltage, while keeping the currents through individual members small and losses negligible. Just some 💭
